A1:
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMBINING TWO ADJECTIVES WITH 'AND': Can use 'and' to join a limited range of common adjectives.
    e.g. The teachers are very nice and friendly .
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'VERY': Can use 'very' with a limited range of common gradable adjectives.
    e.g. I like Croydon because it's very quiet and very nice.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: ATTRIBUTIVE (WITH NOUNS): Can use a limited range of adjectives attributively, before a noun. ► noun phrases
    e.g. We have a big garden and a small swimming pool.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: PREDICATIVE, WITH 'BE': Can use a limited range of adjectives predicatively, after 'be'.
    e.g. I like my home because my bedroom is big.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: 'MY BEST FRIEND': Can use the irregular superlative adjective 'best' in the phrase 'my best friend'.
    e.g. She's my best friend.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: TIME: Can use 'soon' in the phrases 'See you soon' and 'Get well soon', as a signing-off device.
    e.g. Take care. See you soon.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: PLACE: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('here', 'there') to indicate place.
    e.g. My girlfriend is here with me.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: FREQUENCY: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('always', 'sometimes') to talk about frequency.
    e.g. I like Dorchester's people, they are always very friendly and polite.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: 'VERY' WITH ADJECTIVES: Can use 'very' to make adjectives stronger.
    e.g. He's a very good man.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: DEGREE WITH VERBS: Can use 'really' and 'very much' with verbs expressing preference ('like', 'love'), for emphasis.
    e.g. I really like theatre and a classical music.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: 'VERY' + ADJECTIVES: Can use 'very' to modify common gradable adjectives.
    e.g. My home is very nice.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: 'VERY' + TIME ADVERBS: Can use 'very' to modify time adverbs. ► adverb phrases
    e.g. I hope you get well very soon.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: DEGREE and TIME, MODIFYING VERBS: Can use degree and time adverbs to modify verbs.
    e.g. I really like Japanese music.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: PLACE, COMPLEMENT: Can use place adverbs ('here', 'there') as complements of verbs.
    e.g. I am happy here because people are very nice.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: DEGREE, BEFORE ADJECTIVES: Can use a limited range of degree adverbs before an adjective.
    e.g. My home is very light and very comfortable.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: FREQUENCY, MID POSITION: Can use frequency adverbs in the usual mid position, between the subject and the main verb.
    e.g. In my free time I always listen to music, watch TV, clean my room ...
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: FRONT POSITION: Can use a limited range of adverbs in front position.
    e.g. Sometimes I go to the beach.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: 'REALLY,'  MID POSITION: Can use 'really' in the usual mid-position, between the subject and the main verb.
    e.g. I really love walking.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: TIME, END POSITION: Can use time adverbs in the usual end position.
    e.g. Get well soon.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: PLACE, END POSITION: Can use place adverbs in the usual end position.
    e.g. I have many friends there.
[ADVERBS/adverb phrases - form] FORM: 'VERY' + ADVERB: Can modify adverbs of time and degree with 'very'.
    e.g. I hope you will be fine very soon.
[CLAUSES/coordinated] FORM: MAIN CLAUSE + MAIN CLAUSE: Can combine two main declarative clauses using co-ordinating conjunctions ('and', 'but', 'or').
    e.g. I like my college and I like my class.
[CLAUSES/coordinated] FORM: CLAUSE + CLAUSE, ELLIPTED SUBJECT: Can omit the subject in a second main declarative clause when the subject is obvious or the same as the first clause.
    e.g. She likes to listen to music and watch TV.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE DECLARATIVE: Can form simple affirmative declarative clauses.
    e.g. I study English at college.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: NEGATIVE DECLARATIVE WITH 'BE': Can form a simple negative declarative clause with lexical 'be' + 'not' or 'n't'.
    e.g. My college is not very big.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: NEGATIVE DECLARATIVE, LEXICAL VERBS: Can form simple negative declarative clauses.
    e.g. I don't like maths.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE DECLARATIVE, MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS: Can form an affirmative declarative clause with modal verbs.
    e.g. I can go to see her tomorrow.
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE INTERROGATIVE, WITH 'BE': Can form interrogative clauses with 'be'.
    e.g. How are you?
[CLAUSES/subordinated] FORM/USE: 'BECAUSE', REASONS: Can use a finite subordinate clause with 'because', after a main clause, to introduce reasons.
    e.g. I love her because she is friendly.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] FORM: WORD + WORD: Can use single word conjunctions ('and, but, or') to connect single nouns and adjectives.
    e.g. I like chicken and rice. I don't like meat and fish.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] FORM: PHRASE + PHRASE: Can use single word conjunctions ('and, but, or') to combine phrases.
    e.g. My home is very light and very comfortable.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] FORM: CLAUSE + CLAUSE: Can use single word conjunctions ('and, but, or') to combine clauses.
    e.g. He is a journalist and he likes everything about cinema.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] FORM: SENTENCE + SENTENCE: Can use single word conjunctions ('and, but, or') to combine sentences.
    e.g. I like it because there are lots of shops, it is not too busy and there is lots of parking. And I like my home because it's not too big and it's a very nice flat.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] USE: LISTING: Can use 'and' and 'or' before the final item in a list.
    e.g. I like to eat chicken, mushrooms and some vegetables.
[CONJUNCTIONS/coordinating] USE: 'BUT', CONCESSIVE: Can use 'but' to add an unexpected contrast.
    e.g. I really like my home, it is very small but it is very easy to clean.
[CONJUNCTIONS/subordinating] FORM: 'BECAUSE': Can use 'because' as a subordinating conjunction to introduce a subordinate clause.
    e.g. I want a big garden because I have children.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: WITH NOUNS: Can use articles 'the', 'a' and 'an' before nouns. ► noun phrases
    e.g. She lives in a small flat in the town - it's great!
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: 'A' + ADJECTIVES: Can use 'a' and 'an' before adjectives in a noun phrase. ► adjectives ►  noun phrases
    e.g. You can make a quick snack, for example a sandwich.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: 'A' + 'VERY' + ADJECTIVES: Can use 'a' + 'very' + adjectives in basic noun phrases. ► adverbs ►  noun phrases
    e.g. He's a very good man.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: PREPOSITION + 'THE' + NOUN: Can use 'the' in prepositional phrases relating to time.
    e.g. I work at the weekend and I'm very busy from Monday to Friday.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: PREPOSITION + NO ARTICLE: Can use no article before a limited range of nouns in some fixed expressions with prepositions.
    e.g. If you are at home, I can come tomorrow.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM/USE: NO ARTICLE: Can use no article before a limited range of singular, plural and uncountable nouns when referring to things in general.
    e.g. In my free time I usually play football.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THIS' WITH SINGULAR NOUNS: Can use 'this' with singular nouns. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I like this place because is very nice and quiet.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS', POINTING: Can use 'this' in a limited range of contexts to refer to places from the speaker's or writer's point of view. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I want to let you know about this area and the shops near here.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS', FUTURE: Can use 'this' with time and date words to refer to 'the one that's coming'.
    e.g. … this summer I want to spend my holiday in my country, Bulgaria.
[DETERMINERS/possessives] FORM: WITH NOUNS: Can use possessive determiners 'my', 'your', 'his', 'her' and 'our' before nouns. ► noun phrases ► possessive pronouns
    e.g. I live with my family. My home is a flat.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: WITH PLURAL NOUNS: Can use a limited range of quantifying determiners with plural nouns ('some', 'lots of', 'a lot of' and numbers).
    e.g. Near my home there are some shops.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: WITH SINGULAR NOUNS: Can use a limited range of quantifying determiners with singular nouns ('a', 'every').► determiners: articles
    e.g. In my home are 3 bedrooms. I have a big kitchen and small toilet.
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE 'WILL': Can use the affirmative forms 'will' and ''ll'.
    e.g. I will see you soon.
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: PLANS AND INTENTIONS WITH 'WILL': Can use 'will' and ''ll' to talk about plans and intentions.
    e.g. I will try to come to see you tomorrow.
[MODALITY/can] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. You can see a lot of special places in Wroclaw.
[MODALITY/can] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative forms.
    e.g. I cannot come to see you.
[MODALITY/can] FORM: QUESTION: Can use the affirmative question form.
    e.g. Can you come for dinner Monday at 6.00 pm?
[MODALITY/can] USE: ABILITY: Can use 'can' to talk about ability to do something.
    e.g. I can cook.
[MODALITY/can] USE: OFFERS: Can use 'can' to make an offer.
    e.g. Can I help you?
[MODALITY/can] USE: POSSIBILITY: Can use 'can' to talk about possibility.
    e.g. The shop is next to the post office. There you can find milk, juices and sweets but you can't buy meat.
[MODALITY/can] USE: REQUESTS: Can use 'can' to make a request.
    e.g. Can you come on Sunday at seven o’clock?
[MODALITY/will] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative forms.
    e.g. I will be at your house at 7.30 because I have an appointment before.
[MODALITY/will] USE: PLANS AND INTENTIONS: Can use 'will' to talk about plans and intentions.
    e.g. I will bring some music to the dinner.
[MODALITY/would] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE WITH 'LIKE': Can use the affirmative form with 'like'.
    e.g. I'd like to invite you to dinner.
[MODALITY/would] USE: INVITATIONS WITH 'LIKE': Can use 'would like to invite' to make invitations.
    e.g. I would like to invite you to dinner in my house on Saturday at 7 30 pm.
[MODALITY/would] USE: WISHES AND PREFERENCES WITH 'LIKE': Can use 'would like' to talk about wishes and preferences.
    e.g. If possible, I'd like to come to see you on Tuesday afternoon.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: DETERMINER + NOUN: Can form simple noun phrases with a limited range of determiners + singular and plural nouns. ► Nouns and noun phrases: functions
    e.g. There is also a park near my house.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: DETERMINER + ADJECTIVE + NOUN: Can form simple noun phrases by pre-modifying singular and plural nouns with an adjective after a determiner.
    e.g. Martin is my best friend.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: ADJECTIVE + PLURAL NOUN: Can form simple noun phrases by pre-modifying plural nouns with an adjective and no determiner.
    e.g. I have new neighbours.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: NOUN + NOUN: Can form noun phrases by pre-modifying a limited range of nouns with another noun.
    e.g. Every Friday I go swimming and play computer games.
[NOUNS/types] FORM: COMMON NOUNS: Can use a limited range of common nouns.
    e.g. My house is in Ilford.
[NOUNS/types] FORM: PROPER NOUNS: Can use proper nouns with a capital letter to refer to specific people, places, days and things.
    e.g. I want to see you on Saturday and Sunday.
[NOUNS/types] FORM: COMPOUND NOUNS: Can use a range of compound nouns, as one-word, two-word and hyphenated compounds, formed from verb + noun, noun + noun and adjective + noun combinations.
    e.g. She likes to go to the swimming pool and to the gym.
[NOUNS/plural] FORM: PLURAL '-S': Can form plurals by adding '-s' to common countable nouns.
    e.g. We play computer games.
[NOUNS/noun phrases - grammatical functions] FORM: AS SUBJECTS: Can use nouns and noun phrases as subjects of the clause.
    e.g. His name is Stanislav.
[NOUNS/noun phrases - grammatical functions] FORM: AS OBJECTS: Can use nouns and noun phrases as objects of the clause.
    e.g. I love my house.
[NOUNS/noun phrases - grammatical functions] FORM: AS COMPLEMENT OF PREPOSITIONS: Can use nouns and noun phrases as complements of prepositions in prepositional phrases.
    e.g. I invite you and your family to come to my house for dinner.
[NOUNS/noun phrases - grammatical functions] FORM: AS COMPLEMENT OF 'BE': Can use nouns and noun phrases as complements of the verb 'be'.
    e.g. There is a big garden.
[NOUNS/noun phrases - grammatical functions] FORM: AS ADJUNCTS: Can use nouns and noun phrases as adjuncts in some time expressions.
    e.g. But if not, then I will see you tomorrow morning.
[PAST/past simple] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form with a limited range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. Every day at college I went to class at 9 am.
[PAST/past simple] USE: EVERYDAY EVENTS AND STATES: Can use the past simple to talk about everyday events or states.
    e.g. The people were very polite.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: SIMPLE: Can use a limited range of simple (single-word) prepositions.
    e.g. I'm sending some flowers to you.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: PREPOSITION + NOUN PHRASE: Can form prepositional phrases with a preposition and a noun phrase.
    e.g. When I have free time, I go to the park with my dog.
[PRESENT/present continuous] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. I am sending you some flowers, because you are not well.
[PRESENT/present continuous] USE: EVENTS IN PROGRESS: Can use the present continuous with a limited range of verbs to talk about situations and events in progress.
    e.g. [in a message to someone who is ill] We are missing you.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form with a limited range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. Every day at college I learn new words and new things.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form with a limited range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. I don't like meat and fish.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: WITH 'REALLY': Can use the present simple with the adverb 'really' as an intensifier. ► adverbs
    e.g. I really like my kitchen, because it is big and new and it makes my cooking easy.
[PRESENT/present simple] USE: HABITS AND GENERAL FACTS: Can use the present simple to talk about repeated events or habits, and general facts.
    e.g. I work in a supermarket.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (OBJECT) 'IT': Can use the pronoun 'it' as a direct object to refer to an object or situation already referred to.
    e.g. The maths is boring and I don't like it.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (OBJECT) 'ME', 'YOU', 'HER', 'HIM', 'US', 'THEM': Can use the pronouns 'me', 'you', 'him', 'her', 'us' and 'them' in the direct object position after a transitive verb.
    e.g. I'm very happy that you want to see me.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (OBJECT) WITH PREPOSITION: Can use the object pronouns 'me', 'you', 'him', 'her', 'it', 'us' and 'them' in the object position after prepositions.
    e.g. I really like to learn new words. It's very important for me.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) 'IT' FOR FIRST PERSON: Can use the pronoun 'it' before 'be' to refer to a first person speaker or writer.
    e.g. Hello Mrs Bishop. It's Clarisse
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) QUESTIONS: Can use the pronouns 'I', 'you', 'we' and 'they' after an auxiliary or modal verb in question forms.
    e.g. Can I help you?
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) QUESTIONS WITH 'BE': Can use the pronouns 'you' and 'it' after 'be' in question forms.
    e.g. Hi, Mrs. Bishop. How are you?
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) STATEMENTS: Can use the pronouns 'I', 'you', 'he', 'she', 'it', 'we' and 'they' in the subject position before a verb in statements.
    e.g. In my free time I go shopping and I like swimming.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: OBJECT: Can use a limited range of indefinite pronouns as objects.
    e.g. I like doing everything at College because I love my college ?
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: 'EVERYTHING', SUBJECT: Can use 'everything' as subject, with a singular verb.
    e.g. I hope everything is all right.
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: MODAL VERBS: Can use a limited range of modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.  ► can
    e.g. Can you come to dinner in my house?
[VERBS/prepositional] FORM: VERB + PREPOSITION + OBJECT: Can use a limited range of prepositional verbs followed by noun or pronoun objects.
    e.g. I listen to music in the living room, and also eat my breakfast, lunch and dinner.
[VERBS/types] FORM: MAIN VERBS: Can use regular and irregular main verbs in simple verb phrases. ► present simple ► past simple
    e.g. You know, I love your cooking!
[VERBS/types] FORM: LINKING VERB, 'BE': Can use linking verb 'be'.
    e.g. I am 22 years old.
[VERBS/types] FORM: AUXILIARY VERB 'BE': Can use auxiliary verb 'be'. ►  present continuous
    e.g. I am writing to tell you what I do every day at college.
[VERBS/types] FORM: MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS: Can use a limited range of modal auxiliary verbs ('can', 'will', 'would').
    e.g. Can you bring some music with you to the dinner.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: VERB + 'TO'-INFINITIVE: Can use a limited range of verbs followed by a 'to'- infinitive.
    e.g. I like to go to the zoo.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: 'LIKE' + 'TO'-INFINITIVE OR + '-ING': Can use 'like' followed either by a 'to'-infinitive or an '-ing' form, with no change in meaning.
    e.g. I like to work with my teacher.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: 'WOULD LIKE TO': Can use 'would like to' + infinitive.
    e.g. I would like to meet you to give you some information about the Brannl State and the shops near her.
[VERBS/there is/are] FORM: 'THERE IS': Can use 'there is' + singular noun phrase as complement.
    e.g. My home is nice, there is a nice garden.
[VERBS/there is/are] FORM: 'THERE ARE': Can use 'there are' + plural noun phrase as complement.
    e.g. There are two bathrooms.
[VERBS/linking] FORM: 'BE' + COMPLEMENT: Can use linking verb 'be' with complements.
    e.g. Because today is my birthday.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: MAIN VERB 'BE': Can form negative statements of main verb 'be', with contracted and uncontracted forms.
    e.g. My college is not very big.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: AUXILIARY VERB 'DO', PRESENT: Can form negative statements of main verbs in the present simple with 'don't' + main verb. ► present simple ► can
    e.g. I don't have a dining room, so we eat in the living room.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: MODAL VERB 'CAN': Can use negative form 'can't'.
    e.g. In winter, we can't go visiting because it is too cold.
A2:
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMBINING TWO ADJECTIVES WITH 'BUT': Can use 'but' to join a limited range of common adjectives, after 'be'.
    e.g. The weather was cloudy but fine.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: + -ER: Can form comparative adjectives from adjectives of one syllable by adding '-er'.
    e.g. I played computer games with my older brother.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: + -IER: Can form comparative adjectives with adjectives of two syllables ending in '-y' by changing the 'y' to an 'I' and adding '-er'.
    e.g. I like it because it's small and easier to use than other phones.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: BEFORE NOUNS: Can use comparative adjectives attributively, before nouns.
    e.g. I just watched a cricket match with my younger brother and my father.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: COMPLEMENT OF 'BE': Can use comparative adjectives as a complement of 'be'.
    e.g. Sam, It would be better if you came after 4.30 p.m.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: DOUBLE CONSONANT + '-ER': Can form comparative adjectives with adjectives of one syllable with a short vowel, by doubling the final consonant and adding '-er'
    e.g. The rooms are all very beautiful, but I prefer my room because is bigger.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: ENDING IN '-E' + '-R': Can form comparative adjectives with adjectives of one syllable ending in 'e', by adding '-r'.
    e.g. [talking about an alternative plan] I think it will be nicer.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: IRREGULAR: Can form irregular comparative adjectives.
    e.g. What colour do you think is better?
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: WITH 'MORE': Can form a range of comparative adjective phrases using 'more' + longer adjectives (usually three or more syllables).
    e.g. I think you must be more careful.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM: WITH 'THAN': Can use a comparative adjective with 'than' to compare two nouns or noun phrases.► comparative clauses
    e.g. It's smaller than my old phone and I like it because it has bluetooth and it isn't very expensive.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH DEGREE ADVERBS: Can use adverbs of degree ('really', 'so', 'quite') with an increasing range of common gradable adjectives.
    e.g. It was sunny and really hot every day!
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH PREPOSITIONAL PHRASE: Can form adjective phrases with a very limited range of adjectives + a prepositional phrase.
    e.g. I think swimming is good for my body.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'TOO': Can use 'too' with common gradable adjectives.
    e.g. They aren't too expensive. They cost like £30.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'TOO' + PREPOSITIONAL PHRASE: Can use 'too' + adjective + prepositional phrase
    e.g. I bought them because I noticed that most of my clothes were too small for me.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM/USE: LIMITING ADJECTIVES: Can use a limited range of adjectives ('main', 'only') that limit the noun that they go before.
    e.g. …the main reason is that I am practising the language.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: PREDICATIVE, WITH 'BE': Can use an increasing range of adjectives predicatively, after 'be'.
    e.g. Yes, I am free Monday morning.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: PREDICATIVE, WITH LINKING VERBS: Can use a limited range of adjectives predicatively, after linking verbs 'look' and 'feel'.
    e.g. It is black and it looks perfect.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM/USE: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASES: Can form a limited range of complex noun phrases with a superlative adjective  + prepositional phrase, to talk about something unique.► noun phrases ►  clauses: comparison
    e.g. It is the biggest zoo in the world.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM/USE: WITH 'IN' + NOUN: Can use prepositional phrases with 'in' + singular name of a place after a superlative adjective.
    e.g. It's the biggest room in the house.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM/USE: WITH 'OF' + NOUN: Can use a limited range of prepositional phrases with 'of' after a superlative adjective, to refer to one thing within a set or part of a set.
    e.g. My teacher said that it was the best project of all.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: ELLIPSIS, WITH 'THE': Can use 'the' with a limited range of superlative adjectives without a following noun, when the noun is understood.
    e.g. My relatives gave me presents but the computer game was the best.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH '-EST': Can form superlative adjectives with adjectives of one syllable with a short vowel, by doubling the final consonant and adding '–est'.
    e.g. … it's the biggest and oldest museum in Libya.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH '-EST': Can form superlative adjectives by adding the suffix '-est', to adjectives of one syllable.
    e.g. … it's the biggest and oldest museum in Libya.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH '-EST': Can form superlative adjectives using the '-est' suffix, with adjectives of one syllable, and with two-syllable adjectives ending in -y.
    e.g. The easiest way to get my house is by Underground.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH 'MY' OR 'YOUR': Can form a noun phrase with 'my' or 'your' + superlative adjective + noun, with a limited range of nouns and contexts. ► noun phrases
    e.g. I bought these clothes for my youngest brother ....
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH '-ST': Can form superlative adjectives using the suffix '-st' to adjectives ending in '–e'.
    e.g. I always watch the latest music videos from there.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH 'THE': Can form a noun phrase with 'the' + superlative adjective + noun. ► noun phrases
    e.g. It has the latest technology.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM: WITH 'THE MOST': Can form superlative adjective phrases using 'the most', with longer adjectives of two or more syllables.
    e.g. We love football competitions because here in Uruguay football is the most popular sport.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: TIME: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('yesterday', 'just', 'now', 'already',) to refer to the timing of an event.
    e.g. Yesterday I went shopping.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: FREQUENCY: Can use an increasing range of adverbs ('usually', 'often', 'never', 'weekly') to talk about frequency.
    e.g. People usually try to eat their breakfast between 7 and 9 am.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: DEGREE, WITH ADJECTIVES: Can use a limited range of degree adverbs ('so', 'quite', 'too') to modify adjectives.
    e.g. I went to Vinpearland. It's so beautiful.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: DEGREE WITH VERBS: Can use a limited range of adverbs and adverb phrases ('so much', 'a lot') with verbs to indicate degree or intensity. ► determiners
    e.g. I am looking forward to seeing you so much!
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: MANNER: Can use a limited range of manner adverbs and adverb phrases to modify how something happens.
    e.g. … both the players played well.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: LINKING: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('also', 'so', 'however') to show a relationship between two clauses or sentences.
    e.g. I also bought some t-shirts.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: FOCUS: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('only', 'just') to focus on or point to something.
    e.g. [talking about a class] It's only two hours.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: SEQUENCING: Can use a limited range of adverbs and adverb phrases ('first', 'then', 'after that') to order segments of discourse.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: CERTAINTY: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('maybe', 'perhaps') to indicate degrees of certainty.
    e.g. Or maybe we can walk to there, because it's not far from my house.
[ADVERBS/adverbs as modifiers] USE: STANCE: Can use a limited range of adverbs ('actually', 'unfortunately', 'of course', 'I’m sorry') to indicate an attitude or viewpoint.
    e.g. It's very nice that you're going to help me to paint my bedroom. Actually I want you to decide what colour that my room should be.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: DEGREE, MODIFYING ADJECTIVES: Can use a range of degree adverbs to modify common gradable adjectives.
    e.g. I'm so happy because you can come!
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: MANNER, MODIFYING VERBS: Can use a limited range of manner adverbs to modify verbs.
    e.g. I really liked it because all the candidates were professional and swam very fast.
[ADVERBS/adverbs and adverb phrases: types and meanings] FORM: TIME  and SEQUENCING, MODIFYING CLAUSES: Can use time and sequencing adverbs to modify clauses and sentences.
    e.g. Yesterday I went shopping and bought some clothes.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: DEGREE, BEFORE ADJECTIVES: Can use an increasing range of degree adverbs before an adjective.
    e.g. I'm so glad you are coming.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: FRONT POSITION: Can use an increasing range of adverbs in front position.
    e.g. [talking about taking a language course] However I can study only on Monday, Tuesday and Wednesday, because I am working in the restaurant.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: MID POSITION: Can use adverbs in mid position between the subject and the main verb and after modal verbs, auxiliary verbs and 'be'.
    e.g. I bought it because it is very pretty, but it wasn't expensive, I only spent thirty pounds!
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: END POSITION, MANNER: Can use manner adverbs in the usual end position.
    e.g. Please answer me quickly because there are only eleven tickets.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: DEGREE, END POSITION: Can use degree adverbs in end position.
    e.g. I miss you a lot.
[ADVERBS/position] FORM: SENTENCE ADVERB,  FRONT POSITION: Can use evaluative, viewpoint and sequencing adverbs outside the clause, in front position, as a cohesive device to link to a previous statement in a preceding clause.
    e.g. It's very nice that you're going to help me to paint my bedroom. Actually I want you to decide what colour that my room should be.
[ADVERBS/adverb phrases - form] FORM: ADVERB + ADVERB: Can modify a limited range of adverbs of time, degree, and manner with other adverbs.
    e.g. I love it so much.
[CLAUSES/comparatives] FORM: 'BE LIKE' + NOUN OR PRONOUN: Can use 'be' + 'like' + noun or pronoun.
    e.g. I like her because she is like me.
[CLAUSES/coordinated] FORM/USE: MULTIPLE MAIN CLAUSES, INSTRUCTIONS: Can combine multiple main clauses, with commas and conjunctions expressing order, often to give instructions or directions.
    e.g. Your house is near to mine so turn down the 5th street and then go straight almost all the way. When you see the purple street turn there, then go to the left to the Red street, that is my street, my house is 17.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: NEGATIVE DECLARATIVE WITH 'HAVE': Can form a simple negative declarative clause with auxiliary 'have' + 'not' or 'n't' and 'have' + 'not' or 'n't (got)'
    e.g. I haven't eaten Italian food for a long time.
[CLAUSES/declarative] FORM: NEGATIVE DECLARATIVE, MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS: Can form a simple negative declarative clause with modal verb + 'not' or 'n't'.
    e.g. I know you couldn't come to my party so I want to tell you about my presents and party.
[CLAUSES/imperatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can form an affirmative imperative with the base form of a main verb.
    e.g. Goodbye. Write soon.
[CLAUSES/imperatives] USE: INSTRUCTIONS: Can use the imperative to give instructions.
    e.g. Take a taxi to the Petrol station then call me.
[CLAUSES/imperatives] USE: CLOSINGS: Can use the imperative to give instructions, often at the end of a letter or when saying goodbye to someone.
    e.g. Email or phone me.
[CLAUSES/imperatives] FORM/USE: NEGATIVE: Can form a negative imperative with the auxiliary verb 'do' + 'n't' + base form of a main verb, to give advice, instructions or orders.
    e.g. Don't tell Lucy, please.
[CLAUSES/imperatives] FORM/USE: 'LET'S', SUGGESTION: Can use 'let's' + base form of a main verb, for first person plural imperatives to make a suggestion.
    e.g. The concert will be in Canecão. Let's go!!!
[CLAUSES/imperatives] USE: EMPHASISING AN INSTRUCTION: Can use negative imperative after an instruction, for emphasis.
    e.g. Our house is next to the school and my home is opposite the post office. Don't forget.
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE INTERROGATIVE, WITH AUXILIARY 'BE/HAVE': Can form interrogative clauses ('yes/no' forms) with auxiliary 'be' and 'have'.
    e.g. Are you going to the new art class?
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE INTERROGATIVE: Can form interrogative clauses ('yes/no' forms) of main lexical verbs with auxiliary 'do'.
    e.g. Do you remember Julie?
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE INTERROGATIVE, WITH MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS: Can form interrogative clauses ('yes/no' forms) with modal auxiliary verbs.
    e.g. Would you like to come with me?
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE 'WH-' INTERROGATIVE: Can form 'wh-' interrogative clauses with a 'wh-' word as object.
    e.g. When are you coming here?
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: NEGATIVE 'WH-' INTERROGATIVE: Can form negative 'wh-' interrogative clauses.
    e.g. Why don't we watch a film?
[CLAUSES/interrogatives] FORM: NEGATIVE INTERROGATIVE: Can form negative interrogative clauses.
    e.g. Isn't it great?
[CLAUSES/phrases/exclamations] FORM: 'WHAT A …!': Can use 'What a' + noun phrase and 'What a' + noun phrase + clause.
    e.g. What a brilliant idea.
[CLAUSES/phrases/exclamations] FORM: 'WHAT A PITY' + ('THAT') CLAUSE: Can use 'What a pity' + ('that') clause.
    e.g. What a pity that you didn't come.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: NON-DEFINING, SUBJECT, WITH 'WHO': Can use a non-defining relative clause with 'who' as the subject.
    e.g. I watched it with my brother who was also interested in sports.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: DEFINING, SUBJECT, WITH 'WHO': Can use a defining relative clause with 'who' as the subject.
    e.g. It's about a girl who went to the war when she was 22 years old.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: NON-DEFINING, SUBJECT, WITH 'WHICH': Can use a non-defining relative clause with 'which' as the subject,.
    e.g. I bought it in Peter's Mobile shop, which is near to my home.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: DEFINING, SUBJECT, WITH 'WHICH': Can use a defining relative clause with 'which' as the subject.
    e.g. At the end of the game, the KASAS sang to Hagas a piece of music which makes me cry. Suzanna watched with me and she cried alot.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: DEFINING, OBJECT, WITH 'THAT': Can use a defining relative clause with 'that' as the object.
    e.g. The team that I like is called 'The Monkeys', but Paty prefers 'The Walls'.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: NON-DEFINING, OBJECT, WITH 'WHICH': Can use a non-defining relative clause with 'which' as the object.
    e.g. The best of all was a marble statue which my aunt gave me.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: DEFINING, OBJECT, WITH 'WHICH': Can use a defining relative clause with 'which' as the object.
    e.g. I came to your house yesterday with the computer game which I borrowed from Mayank.
[CLAUSES/relative] FORM: DEFINING, WITHOUT PRONOUN: Can use a defining relative clause, without a relative pronoun.
    e.g. My friends said the clothes I wore were very old, and they didn't like them.
[CLAUSES/subordinated] FORM/USE: 'IF' CLAUSES, CONDITIONS: Can use a finite subordinate clause with 'if', before or after a main clause, to introduce conditions. ► clauses: conditionals
    e.g. I would be very happy if you visit me in Dublin.
[CLAUSES/subordinated] FORM/USE: TIME: Can use a finite subordinate clause with time conjunctions, before or after a main clause.
    e.g. I feel comfortable when I am wearing these clothes.
[CLAUSES/subordinated] FORM/USE: PURPOSE: Can use a non-finite subordinate clause with 'to', to introduce purpose.
    e.g. To get to my house you'll have to take the 5th avenue and go 6 streets to the left and at the bottom you'll see another street, go 3 streets to the right and there you'll find my house.
[CONJUNCTIONS/subordinating] FORM: SIMPLE: Can use a limited range of simple subordinating conjunctions '(if, when, so, while)' to introduce a subordinate clause.
    e.g. But if you don't take your car, you can rent a bike in the park too.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM/USE: 'THE' + ADJECTIVES, SPECIFYING: Can use 'the' + adjectives in a noun phrase, to specify.
    e.g. My house is opposite the new café.
[DETERMINERS/articles] FORM: 'THE' + SUPERLATIVES: Can form a noun phrase with 'the' + superlative adjectives + noun.
    e.g. It has the latest technology.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THIS' WITH UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS: Can use 'this' with uncountable nouns. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I found this information in: www.artschool.com,
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS' POINTING: Can use 'this' in an increasing range of contexts to refer to places and things from the speaker's or writer's point of view. ► noun phrases ► pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I am writing this letter to tell you about my favourite birthday present, which was a laptop.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: POINTING: Can use 'this' in an increasing range of contexts to refer to places and things from the speaker's or writer's point of view. ► noun phrases ► pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I am writing this letter to tell you about my favourite birthday present which was a laptop.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'this' to refer to something with immediate relevance which has already been mentioned. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. Could you send someone who can check where the problem is and try to do something about this problem.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THAT': Can use 'that' with singular nouns. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I also want to see that film.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THAT', POINTING: Can use 'that' to talk about or point to things which are further away in time and space from the speaker or writer
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THAT', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'that' to refer to something which has already been mentioned. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. Yesterday, I went shopping to Collesione for clothes. I bought a skirt and a T-shirt, because we have a party this week. They were very cheap. They cost £10. You should go that shop.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THESE': Can use 'these' with plural nouns. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I bought these clothes because they weren't expensive.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THESE' POINTING: Can use 'these' to refer to places and things from the speaker's or writer's point of view.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THESE', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'these' to refer to things with immediate relevance or which have already been mentioned. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. … you can visit the Old Museum, the Green Park, the old bridge and old church. These places are very old.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THOSE': Can use 'those' with plural nouns. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. The cost of those clothes was £300.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THOSE', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'those' to refer to things which have already been mentioned. ► noun phrases ►  pronouns: demonstrative
    e.g. I went shopping for clothes yesterday. I bought some jumpers and trousers. I bought those clothes because I'm going to Japan next Friday and there the weather is cold.
[DETERMINERS/demonstratives] USE: 'THOSE' POINTING: Can use 'those' to talk about or point to things which the speaker or writer perceives to be further away in time and space.
[DETERMINERS/possessives] FORM: SINGULAR NOUN +''S': Can use ''s' after singular or proper nouns to indicate possession.
    e.g. It's very important for me because it's my sister's jacket.
[DETERMINERS/possessives] FORM: WITH QUANTIFYING DETERMINERS + 'OF': Can use quantifying determiners + possessive determiners + 'of' + noun. ► noun phrases
    e.g. All of my friends were there.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: WITH PLURAL NOUNS: Can use an increasing range of quantifying determiners with plural nouns ('all', 'both', 'a few').
    e.g. This notice is for all students: we are going to have a concert at school.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: WITH PLURAL AND UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS: Can use a range of quantifying determiners ('some', 'any', 'no', 'more', 'a lot of') with both plural nouns and uncountable nouns.
    e.g. I think we should bring some water to drink.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: WITH SINGULAR NOUNS: Can use an increasing range of quantifying determiners with singular nouns ('each', 'an', 'one' and numbers).
    e.g. The new art class starts next Monday and each class is about 1 hour long.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: 'MUCH' WITH UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS, NEGATIVE: Can use 'much' with uncountable nouns in negative contexts.
    e.g. But we didn't have much money, so we didn't buy many things.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: 'MANY' WITH PLURAL NOUNS, NEGATIVE: Can use 'many' with plural nouns in negative contexts.
    e.g. There aren't many tickets.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: 'SO MANY' WITH PLURAL NOUNS: Can use 'so many' and 'too many' with plural nouns.
    e.g. We played so many games, like dog and bone, four corners, and many more.
[DETERMINERS/quantity] FORM: DETERMINER + 'OF' + DETERMINER: Can use a range of quantifying determiners + 'of' + determiner ('all of', 'some of', 'both of', 'many of', 'any of', number + 'of', 'each of'). ► pronouns: quantity
    e.g. I spent all of my money!
[FUTURE/future continuous] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE WITH 'WILL': Can use the affirmative form with 'will'.
    e.g. I'll be waiting for you.
[FUTURE/future continuous] USE: FUTURE ARRANGEMENTS: Can use the future continuous with 'will' to talk about an event or action in progress at a specified time in the future.
    e.g. Tomorrow please come at 4.30 pm because I'll be working before.
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE 'SHALL': Can use the affirmative form 'shall' with 'I' and 'we'.
    e.g. I shall come with you.
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] FORM: NEGATIVE 'WILL': Can use the negative forms 'will not' and 'won't'.
    e.g. Without them I will not be able to play football.
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question forms of 'will' and 'shall'.
    e.g. Will you come?
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: OFFERS WITH 'SHALL': Can use 'shall I' to make offers.
    e.g. Shall I wait for you?
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: PLANS AND  INTENTIONS WITH 'WILL': Can use 'will' to ask about plans and intentions.
    e.g. Will you have a mobile phone?
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: REQUESTS WITH 'WILL': Can use 'will' to make requests.
    e.g. Will you come tomorrow at 6.35 pm to my house?
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: SUGGESTIONS WITH 'SHALL': Can use 'shall we' to make suggestions.
    e.g. Shall we go to my friend's house to get it?
[FUTURE/future with will and shall] USE: WILLINGNESS WITH 'WILL': Can use 'will' to express willingness.
    e.g. My mother will drive us there.
[FUTURE/future with be going to] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. [about some new jeans] I 'm going to wear them on holiday.
[FUTURE/future with be going to] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form.
    e.g. What are you going to wear?
[FUTURE/future with be going to] USE: INTENTIONS: Can use the affirmative and question forms of 'be going to' talk about plans and intentions.
    e.g. I'm going to use pink paint.
[FUTURE/future with be going to] USE: PREDICTIONS WITH 'BE': Can use 'be going to' with 'be' to make predictions.
    e.g. I think this Saturday is going to be a nice day.
[FUTURE/present continuous for future use] USE: FUTURE ARRANGEMENTS: Can use the present continuous with a limited range of verbs to talk about future arrangements.
    e.g. I'm working this Saturday and Sunday morning.
[FUTURE/present continuous for future use] USE: QUESTIONS ABOUT THE FUTURE: Can use the present continuous with a limited range of verbs to ask about future plans.
    e.g. [talking about a Rolling Stones concert] Are you coming?
[FUTURE/present simple for future use] FORM: WITH 'WHEN': Can use the present simple with 'when' to refer to the future.
    e.g. I'll tell you more when I meet you.
[FUTURE/present simple for future use] USE: 'HOPE': Can use the present simple with 'I hope' to talk about hopes and wishes for the future.
    e.g. I hope you enjoy your holiday too.
[FUTURE/present simple for future use] USE: TIMETABLES AND PLANS: Can use the present simple to talk about timetabled events in the future.
    e.g. [Talking about a film] It begins on 8 o'clock in the evening.
[MODALITY/adjectives] FORM: 'BE' + 'SURE' + CLAUSE: Can use 'I' + 'be' + 'sure' + clause.
    e.g. I'm sure I can beat you at chess.
[MODALITY/can] USE: PERMISSION: Can use 'can' to give and refuse permission or talk about what is forbidden.
    e.g. You can wear anything you want.
[MODALITY/expressions with be] FORM/USE: 'WILL BE ABLE TO': Can use 'will' + 'be able to' + infinitive to talk about possibility and ability.
    e.g. Will you be able to come this time?
[MODALITY/have (got) to] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative forms.
    e.g. This weekend I have to go to a party.
[MODALITY/have (got) to] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative forms.
    e.g. You don't have to bring many things with you.
[MODALITY/have (got) to] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question forms.
    e.g. What do you have to wear?
[MODALITY/have (got) to] USE: OBLIGATION: Can use 'have (got) to' to talk about obligations.
    e.g. The concert starts at midnight but we have to go before then because we have got to buy our tickets.
[MODALITY/may] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. [talking about clothes] So I may need them.
[MODALITY/may] USE: POSSIBILITY: Can use 'may' to talk about weak possibility referring to the present and the future.
    e.g. [talking about an important document] It may be in our room inside my bag.
[MODALITY/might] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. The weather might be hot and sunny.
[MODALITY/might] USE: POSSIBILITY: Can use 'might' to talk about weak possibility.
    e.g. My dad isn’t working that day, he might take us.
[MODALITY/must] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. You must wear your sports shoes and you must also bring your racket!
[MODALITY/must] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative forms.
    e.g. … you mustn't forget to buy some strawberry juice for us.
[MODALITY/must] USE: OBLIGATION AND NECESSITY: Can use 'must' with a limited range of pronouns ('I' and 'you') to talk about obligation and necessity.
    e.g. [talking about a mobile phone] It is very important to me because I must call my parents.
[MODALITY/shall] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. We shall start tomorrow at 2 o'clock.
[MODALITY/shall] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form.
    e.g. Shall we meet at half past 4 in front of my house?
[MODALITY/shall] USE: OFFERS: Can use 'shall I' to make offers.
    e.g. Shall I go and buy some cake and crackers?
[MODALITY/shall] USE: SUGGESTIONS: Can use 'shall' with 'we' to make suggestions.
    e.g. Tomorrow evening there is Laura Pausini's concert in Turin. Shall we go?
[MODALITY/should] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. You should come at 6 o'clock.
[MODALITY/should] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form.
    e.g. You shouldn't miss it.
[MODALITY/should] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form.
    e.g. Should I wear special clothes?
[MODALITY/should] USE: ADVICE: Can use 'should' to give advice.
    e.g. It was only €70. It is pink. It looks very nice. My advice, you should buy this.
[MODALITY/should] USE: SUGGESTIONS: Can use 'should' to make suggestions.
    e.g. I think we should meet at 2 pm, and my mother will take us there so don't worry.
[MODALITY/will] FORM/USE: AFTER 'IF', FUTURE SITUATIONS: Can use 'will' in the main clause after an 'if-' clause to talk about possible future situations. ► conditionals
    e.g. If I need a camera, I will use my mobile phone.
[MODALITY/will] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative forms.
    e.g. Don't worry, you won't need anything apart from some pencils and your art book.
[MODALITY/will] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form.
    e.g. Will you be at home?
[MODALITY/will] USE: PLANS AND INTENTIONS: Can use 'will' to ask about plans and intentions.
    e.g. Will you come by bus?
[MODALITY/will] USE: WILLINGNESS, OFFERS: Can use 'will' to talk about willingness and offers
    e.g. My mother will get us there, and she will bring us to my house after too.
[MODALITY/will] USE: REQUESTS: Can use 'will' to make requests in a limited range of contexts.
    e.g. Will you come with me?
[MODALITY/would] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. I'd love to have a pen-friend in Japan.
[MODALITY/would] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative forms.
    e.g. [talking about clothes] You wouldn't believe how much they cost!
[MODALITY/would] FORM: QUESTIONS WITH 'LIKE': Can use the question form 'would you like'.
    e.g. Sarah, would you like to play tennis this evening?
[MODALITY/would] USE: IMAGINED SITUATIONS: Can use 'would' to talk about imagined situations.
    e.g. I was thinking of painting my bedroom blue. It would look awesome.
[MODALITY/would] USE: SUGGESTIONS WITH 'IT WOULD BE': Can use 'it would be' to make suggestions.
    e.g. [talking about a jazz concert] It would be very good if you came with me.
[MODALITY/would] USE: WISHES AND PREFERENCES: Can use 'would' with a wide range of verbs to talk about wishes and preferences.
    e.g. I would prefer to go on Sunday, because on Sunday I have no school.
[MODALITY/adverbs] FORM/USE: MID POSITION, HEDGING OR EMPHASIS: Can use adverbs in mid position or after main verb 'be', to modify an assertion, either through hedging or emphasis. ► adverbs: position
    e.g. It is probably in the sitting room on the table.
[MODALITY/adverbs] FORM: CLAUSE POSITION: Can use adverbs of certainty and possibility ('maybe', 'perhaps'), before a clause or ellipted clause. ► adverbs: position
    e.g. I think I left my phone there, maybe in your kitchen.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: DETERMINER + UNCOUNTABLE NOUN: Can form simple noun phrases with a limited range of determiners + uncountable nouns.
    e.g. The food was delicious and the music was great.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: DETERMINER + NOUN: Can form simple noun phrases by pre-modifying nouns with an increasing range of determiners.
    e.g. There are so many shops, you can play games, eat food like Chinese food.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: NOUN PHRASES WITH ADJECTIVES: Can pre-modify noun phrases with a limited range of more than one adjective.
    e.g. I bought a beautiful pink skirt and a white top.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: WITH RELATIVE CLAUSE AS POSTMODIFIER: Can post-modify noun phrases by using a non-defining relative clause. ► Clauses: relative
    e.g. She's in a volleyball team like me, but we're not in the same one. She was for the other team, which lost.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: WITH RELATIVE CLAUSE AS COMPLEMENT: Can form complex noun phrases by using a defining relative clause as a complement to a noun phrase. ► Clauses: relative
    e.g. The mobile phone which I bought yesterday is brilliant.
[NOUNS/noun phrases] FORM: POSSESSION WITH ''S' + NOUN: Can use possessive determiner ''s' with a singular noun.
    e.g. It's my sister's jacket.
[NOUNS/types] FORM: '-ING' FORMS, OBJECT: Can use the '-ing' forms of verbs as nouns, in object position, to refer to activities.
    e.g. There we did a lot of things like swimming from the beach and going shopping.
[NOUNS/plural] FORM: PLURAL '-ES': Can form plurals by adding '-es' to countable nouns ending in '-o', '-ch', '-s', '-sh', '-x' or '-z'.
    e.g. The art classes after school are on Tuesdays.
[NOUNS/plural] FORM: PLURAL '-IES': Can form plurals of countable nouns ending in a consonant plus '-y', by changing the 'y' to 'I' and adding '-es'.
    e.g. I have a lot of hobbies.
[NOUNS/plural] FORM: IRREGULAR PLURAL NOUNS: Can form some irregular plural nouns.
    e.g. It was a sale and I bought some clothes for my children.
[NOUNS/plural] FORM: NOUNS ONLY USED IN THE PLURAL: Can use nouns that only have a plural form, with plural agreement.
    e.g. I bought them because I feel comfortable when I am wearing these clothes.
[NOUNS/uncountable] FORM: UNCOUNTABLE NOUNS: Can use a limited range of uncountable nouns.
    e.g. I listen to music, I watch TV and I talk to my friends.
[NOUNS/uncountable] FORM: WITH 'THE': Can use uncountable nouns with 'the' to talk about something specific.
    e.g. The music is going to be loud, since it's rap.
[NOUNS/uncountable] FORM: WITH DETERMINERS, QUANTITY: Can use uncountable nouns with a limited range of quantity words and phrases including 'some', 'any', 'a lot of', 'more'. ► Determiners: quantity
    e.g. It's really important for me, because I need to do some homework.
[NOUNS/uncountable] FORM: CONTAINERS, QUANTITY EXPRESSIONS: Can refer to an individual example or quantity of an uncountable noun using words for containers and countable items.
    e.g. We have to bring a bottle of water, trainers and a T-shirt or top.
[PASSIVES/passives: form] FORM/USE: WITH 'BY' TO ADD INFORMATION: Can use the passive with 'by' to add information about something already known.
    e.g. It was bought by my uncle.
[PASSIVES/passives: form] FORM: PAST SIMPLE, AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the past simple passive affirmative after a singular subject.
    e.g. It was built in 1880.
[PASSIVES/passives: form] FORM: PRESENT SIMPLE, AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the present simple passive affirmative with a singular subject.
    e.g. The group is called "playmo".
[PAST/past continuous] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. I was waiting for you all Saturday evening.
[PAST/past continuous] FORM: WITH ADVERBS: Can use the past continuous with a limited range of adverbs in the normal mid position.
    e.g. I was just watching a football match.
[PAST/past continuous] USE: BACKGROUND EVENTS: Can use the past continuous to show that an event was happening in the background to the main event.
    e.g. Yesterday we were studying maths in your house, and I left my book on your bed.
[PAST/past continuous] USE: EVENTS IN PROGRESS: Can use the past continuous to talk about actions and states in progress around a particular time in the past.
    e.g. I went to a soccer game, they were playing the last game to get the championship.
[PAST/past simple] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form with an increasing range of verbs.
    e.g. I forgot to tell you some details about tomorrow evening.
[PAST/past simple] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form with a limited range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. The player from China did not look very happy though.
[PAST/past simple] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use 'yes/no' and 'wh-' question forms with 'you' with a limited range of verbs.
    e.g. Did you buy something yesterday?
[PAST/past simple] FORM: WITH 'WHEN': Can use 'when' + past simple in subordinate clauses.
    e.g. When I arrived, the weather was horrible.
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM/USE: TIME WITH 'FOR': Can use the negative form with 'for' to talk about a past event or action which hasn't occurred again in the period of time up to now.
    e.g. I haven't eaten Italian food for a long time.
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM/USE: WITH 'YET': Can use the negative form with 'yet' to talk about events which are expected to be completed at some point in the future.
    e.g. [talking about a new mobile phone] I haven't seen it yet .
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form 'have' + '-ed' with pronouns 'I' and 'we', and with a limited range of verbs.
    e.g. I have seen the Eiffel Tower and many old buildings.
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form 'haven't' + '-ed' with 'I' with a limited range of verbs.
    e.g. I haven't eaten Italian food for a long time.
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form 'have you' + '-ed' with with a limited range of verbs.
    e.g. Have you bought anything?
[PAST/present perfect simple] FORM: WITH ADVERBS: Can use the present perfect simple with a limited range of adverbs in the normal mid position.
    e.g. I have never seen a competition like that before, and I love it.
[PAST/present perfect simple] USE: EXPERIENCES: Can use the present perfect simple to talk about experiences up to now.
    e.g. I've seen the British Museum.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: SIMPLE: Can use an increasing range of simple (single-word) prepositions.
    e.g. Please return it by tomorrow morning.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: COMPLEX: Can use a limited range of complex prepositions.
    e.g. We can meet in the park near to my house at 7.00 p.m.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: 'JUST' + PREPOSITION: Can use 'just' to modify prepositions.
    e.g. It's just near the cinema.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: + '-ING': Can form prepositional phrases using the '-ing' form of verbs as complements.
    e.g. I can help by bringing the sound box and bit box I am free at friday 7:30 pm.
[PREPOSITIONS/prepositions] FORM: STRANDING: Can end a 'wh-' question with a preposition, where the preposition is stranded from its complement.
    e.g. Who are you going with?
[PRESENT/present continuous] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form.
    e.g. [talking about a computer] I?m not using it any more.
[PRESENT/present continuous] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use 'wh-' questions, especially in the context of letters and emails.
    e.g. Dear Mom, How are you doing?
[PRESENT/present continuous] FORM/USE: WITH ADVERBS OF INDEFINITE FREQUENCY: Can use the present continuous with a limited range of adverbs of indefinite frequency, often to talk about surprising or undesirable situations or events (especially with 'always').
    e.g. I'm always dreaming about a job as a secretary.
[PRESENT/present continuous] USE: EVENTS IN PROGRESS: Can use the present continuous with an increasing range of verbs to talk about situations and events in progress.
    e.g. I am watching a swimming competition.
[PRESENT/present continuous] USE: TEMPORARY SITUATIONS: Can use the present continuous with a limited range of verbs to talk about temporary situations.
    e.g. [talking about a temporary situation] I'm living with my friend in Wolverhampton. I'm studying at Wolverhampton College.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM/USE: REAL AND IMAGINED SITUATIONS AFTER 'IF': Can use the present simple after 'if' to talk about real and imagined situations.
    e.g. If you want some help, I will be free next week.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form with an increasing range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. They are very important to me, because they bring me good luck.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: INDIRECT QUESTIONS: Can form indirect questions with 'Do you know' + 'how', 'where', 'why', or 'what' ...?'
    e.g. Do you know how to get to my house?
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form with an increasing range of regular and irregular verbs.
    e.g. I don't need the video because I've got the DVD.
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use 'yes/no' and 'wh-' question forms with 'you'.
    e.g. Do you have a mobile phone?
[PRESENT/present simple] FORM: WITH ADVERBS OF INDEFINITE FREQUENCY: Can use the present simple with adverbs of indefinite frequency.    ► adverbs
    e.g. People usually try to eat their breakfast between 7 and 9 am.
[PRESENT/present simple] USE: INSTRUCTIONS AND DIRECTIONS: Can use the present simple to give instructions and directions.
    e.g. Then you turn right and my house is on your left.
[PRESENT/present simple] USE: MENTAL PROCESS VERBS: Can use the present simple with a limited range of mental process verbs including 'think', 'hope', 'know'.
    e.g. I think my bag is in your kitchen.
[PRESENT/present simple] USE: SUGGESTIONS: Can use the present simple with 'why don't you...?', 'why don't we...?' and 'if you want or if you like' to make suggestions.
    e.g. Why don't you wear your black suit?
[PRONOUNS/possessive] FORM: + 'OF MINE': Can use the possessive pronoun 'mine' after 'of' in the phrases 'a friend of mine' and 'friends of mine'.
    e.g. A friend of mine gave me a present for my birthday.
[PRONOUNS/possessive] FORM: 'MINE': Can use the possessive pronoun 'mine', with singular and plural reference, in subject and object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
    e.g. I bought a new jacket because mine was very old.
[PRONOUNS/possessive] FORM: 'YOURS' AS OBJECT: Can use the possessive pronoun 'yours', with singular reference, in object positions, and complement positions after 'be' and after prepositions.
    e.g. It was my best present, although I also loved yours.
[PRONOUNS/reflexive] FORM: SAME SUBJECT AND OBJECT.: Can use a limited range of singular reflexive pronouns ('myself', 'yourself') with a limited range of verbs ('enjoy', 'buy') to refer to actions where the subject and object of the verb are the same.
    e.g. I enjoyed myself so much.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) ELLIPSIS AFTER 'HOPE': Can leave out the subject pronoun with 'hope'. ► ellipsis
    e.g. Hope to see you soon.
[PRONOUNS/subject/ object] FORM: (SUBJECT) EMPTY 'IT': Can use 'it' as an empty subject when there is nothing else to put in subject position, particularly with weather or time.
    e.g. It's warm and windy.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THIS': Can use 'this' as a pronoun with singular reference.
    e.g. This is a very interesting place.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS' POINTING: Can use 'this' as a pronoun to point to or identify single things or entities which are about to follow and have immediate relevance.
    e.g. This is my number: 0754 567 78 51.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'this' as a pronoun to refer back to single things or groups of plural things which have already been mentioned.
    e.g. Thank you for this.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] USE: 'THIS', NOW: Can use 'this' as a pronoun to refer to something that is happening now.
    e.g. This is the best holiday I have ever had!
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] FORM: 'THAT': Can use 'that' as a pronoun with singular reference.
    e.g. That was amazing.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] USE: 'THAT', ALREADY MENTIONED: Can use 'that' as a pronoun to refer back to something which has already been mentioned.
    e.g. You know that blue is my favourite colour, so we are going to use that.
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] FORM/USE: RESPONSE TOKENS WITH 'THAT'S': Can use 'that's' + adjective to respond to something.
    e.g. That's great!
[PRONOUNS/demonstratives] USE: CLOSING: Can use 'that's all' to end a letter.
    e.g. That's all for now.
[PRONOUNS/quantity] FORM: 'SOME' AND 'ANY': Can use pronouns 'some' and 'any', to refer to quantity.
    e.g. [talking about items of clothing] Some were cheap but some were expensive.
[PRONOUNS/quantity] FORM: 'SOME' AS SUBJECT AND OBJECT: Can use 'some' as a subject and object to refer back to a plural or uncountable noun.
    e.g. I saw a lot of people on the beach, some were playing football and some were sunbathing. (A2 WAYSTAGE
[PRONOUNS/quantity] FORM: 'ANY' AS OBJECT: Can use 'any' as an object in a negative clause to refer back to a plural or uncountable noun.
    e.g. Bring some good CDs. I don't have any.
[PRONOUNS/quantity] FORM: WITH 'OF' + OBJECT PRONOUN: Can use a limited range of pronouns ('all', 'both') with 'of' followed by an object pronoun, to refer to quantity.
    e.g. There are a lot of functions but I don't know how to use all of them.
[PRONOUNS/substitution, one, ones, none] FORM: 'ONE': Can use 'one' as a pronoun to substitute for singular countable nouns which have already been mentioned or are obvious from the context.
    e.g. [talking about a phone] I chose the black one with the numbers in orange.
[PRONOUNS/substitution, one, ones, none] FORM: PRE-MODIFYING: Can use determiners + premodifers + 'one'.
    e.g. I like my present very much because I needed a new one.
[PRONOUNS/substitution, one, ones, none] FORM/USE: POSTMODIFYING, SPECIFYING: Can use 'the one' and 'the' + pre-modifier + 'one' with a complement, to refer to something specific.
    e.g. My house is No.8 and it's the one with silver gates.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: OBJECT OR COMPLEMENT: Can use an increasing range of indefinite pronouns as objects or complements of prepositions.
    e.g. [talking about a phone] I need it because I have to call someone.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: SUBJECT: Can use a limited range of indefinite pronouns ('someone', 'everyone') as subjects, with a singular verb.
    e.g. I bought them because someone stole my luggage at the airport.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: NEGATIVE + 'ANYTHING': Can use 'anything' after a negative verb form.
    e.g. You don't have to bring anything.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM/USE: WITH ADJECTIVE, SPECIFYING: Can modify 'something' or 'anything' with an adjective, to make it more specific.
    e.g. Next weekend it's my mum's birthday party, so I needed something new to wear.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM/USE: WITH 'TO' INFINITIVE, SPECIFYING: Can modify 'something' or 'anything' with 'to' infinitive, to make it more specific.
    e.g. Please, bring something to drink.
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] FORM: WITH 'ELSE': Can use indefinite pronouns with 'else'.
    e.g. Have you got something else to do on Saturday or can you come with me?
[PRONOUNS/indefinite - thing, -one, -body etc] USE: 'SOMETHING' IN VAGUE EXPRESSIONS: Can use 'something' in vague expressions, to refer to things in a non-specific way.
    e.g. After the concert we can have a pizza or something else.
[PRONOUNS/generic use] USE: 'YOU', GENERAL: Can use 'you' to refer to people in general.
    e.g. My new mobile phone can do lots of things! You can play games, you can take pictures.
[QUESTIONS/alternatives] FORM: WORD + WORD: Can form alternative questions with two words from the same class combined with 'or'.
    e.g. Can you bring some food or drinks for the dinner?
[QUESTIONS/tags] FORM: QUESTION TAGS: Can use a limited range of question tags.
    e.g. You don't know where I live, do you?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM: MAIN VERB 'BE': Can use 'wh-'words + main verb 'be' + subject to form 'wh-' questions.
    e.g. How was your dinner yesterday?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM: WITH AUXILIARY 'DO': Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'do' + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions. ► present simple;  ► past simple
    e.g. What do you think?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM: WITH AUXILIARY 'HAVE': Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'have' to form 'wh-'questions. ► present perfect  ►  past perfect
    e.g. How have you been?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM: WITH AUXILIARY 'BE': Can use 'wh-'words + auxiliary 'be' to form 'wh-'questions. ► present continuous ► past continuous
    e.g. Who are you going with?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM: MODAL VERBS: Can use 'wh-'words + modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'wh-'questions.
    e.g. What will you bring?
[QUESTIONS/wh-] FORM/USE: NEGATIVE QUESTONS, SUGGESTIONS: Can use negative question 'why don't' + pronoun + verb, to make a suggestion or invitation.
    e.g. Why don't we watch a film?
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: MAIN VERB 'BE': Can use main verb 'be' + subject to form 'yes/no' questions.
    e.g. Are you ok?
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: MODAL VERBS: Can use an increasing range of modal verbs + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.
    e.g. Could you meet me at my home at 7 pm?
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: LEXICAL VERBS WITH 'DO': Can use auxiliary 'do' + subject + main verb to form 'yes/no' questions.
    e.g. My favourite song is 'Viva la vida'. Do you know it?
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: AUXILIARY 'BE': Can use auxiliary 'be' + subject + the '-ing' form (continuous form) to form 'yes/no' questions.
    e.g. Are you going to come?
[QUESTIONS/yes/no] FORM: AUXILIARY 'HAVE': Can use auxiliary 'have' + subject + the '-ed' form to form 'yes/no' questions.  ►  present perfect
    e.g. I've just watched a football competition with my family and friends. Have you watched it?
[VERBS/phrasal-prepositional] FORM/USE: 'LOOK FORWARD TO': Can use 'look forward to' as a fixed expression followed by an '-ing' form or noun phrase, usually at the end of correspondence.
    e.g. I'm looking forward to seeing you!
[VERBS/patterns_that clauses] FORM: REPORTING VERBS + DIRECT OBJECT 'THAT'-CLAUSE: Can use a limited range of verbs, typically reporting, with a 'that'-clause as the direct object.
    e.g. I hope that you'll come.
[VERBS/patterns_that clauses] FORM: VERBS + DIRECT OBJECT CLAUSE WITHOUT 'THAT': Can use reporting verbs, especially mental process verbs, with a clause as the direct object, without 'that', especially in informal contexts.
    e.g. I hope you can go, buddy.
[VERBS/patterns_that clauses] FORM: VERBS + INDIRECT OBJECT CLAUSE: Can use verbs, typically reporting verbs, followed by a noun or pronoun as the indirect object and a clause with or without 'that', as the direct object.
    e.g. I told you that she has a new car?
[VERBS/phrasal] FORM: NO OBJECT: Can use a limited range of phrasal verbs without an object.
    e.g. I'm going to get up early on Saturday.
[VERBS/phrasal] FORM: VERB + PRONOUN + PARTICLE: Can use a limited range of phrasal verbs + object pronoun + particle.
    e.g. I will pick you up and arrange everything.
[VERBS/types] FORM: AUXILIARY VERBS 'HAVE' AND 'DO': Can use auxiliary verbs 'have' and 'do'.
    e.g. It has been a long time since I saw you.
[VERBS/types] FORM: LINKING VERBS + ADJECTIVE: Can use linking verbs with adjective complements.
    e.g. It makes me feel comfortable and the room looks bigger with the colour.
[VERBS/types] FORM: MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS: Can use an increasing range of modal auxiliary verbs.
    e.g. And I think we should go somewhere else to have a drink after the film.
[VERBS/types] FORM: SEMI-MODAL AUXILIARY VERBS, 'HAVE (GOT) TO': Can use semi-modal auxiliary verb 'have (got) to'.
    e.g. You've got to bring paintbrushes, paper and paint, that's all.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: VERB + 'TO'- INFINITIVE: Can use an increasing range of verbs followed by a 'to'-infinitive.
    e.g. I bought them because I hope to go to England this year and the weather isn't very good.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: VERBS + 'TO'-INFINITIVE OR + '-ING': Can use verbs expressing preference followed either by a 'to'-infinitive or an '-ing' form, with no change in meaning.
    e.g. I love to play football.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM/USE: 'WOULD LOVE' + 'TO': Can use 'would love to' followed by the 'to'-infinitive to give emphasis.
    e.g. I'd love to do the concert.
[VERBS/patterns_with to and -ing] FORM: 'ENJOY' + '-ING': Can use 'enjoy' followed by the '-ing' form.
    e.g. We enjoy playing the piano and singing together.
[VERBS/there is/are] FORM: 'THERE IS' + UNCOUNTABLE: Can use 'there is' + uncountable nouns as complement.
    e.g. I need it because there is some money in it which I have to pay to someone.
[VERBS/there is/are] FORM: 'THERE IS/ARE' + A LOT OF: Can use both 'there is' and 'there are' + 'a lot of' + plural noun.
    e.g. There's a lot of people there.
[VERBS/linking] FORM: LINKING + COMPLEMENT: Can use an increasing range of linking verbs with complements.
    e.g. It looks good on you.
[CLAUSES/conditional] FORM: 'IF' + PRESENT SIMPLE: Can form conditional subordinate clause with 'if' + present simple.
    e.g. It is 3 hours long, and if you want to pass it you will have to study very hard, it is very difficult.
[CLAUSES/conditional] FORM: PRESENT SIMPLE 'IF' CLAUSE + IMPERATIVE: Can use 'if' + present simple, with an imperative in the main clause.
    e.g. If you find it, call me.
[CLAUSES/conditional] FORM/USE: PRESENT SIMPLE 'IF' CLAUSE, REAL CONDITIONS: Can use 'if' + present simple with present simple, 'can' or imperative in the main clause to refer to things that are true now or very likely to happen.
    e.g. If I have spare time I always read a book.
[CLAUSES/conditional] USE: 'IF' CLAUSE + IMPERATIVE, HEDGING: Can use an 'if-' clause ('if you want', 'like', 'prefer') to soften the directness of imperatives, offers or suggestions.
    e.g. Bring some films if you want.
[DISCOURSE MARKERS/discourse markers in writing] FORM/USE: 'AS YOU KNOW', ORGANISING, MARKING SHARED KNOWLEDGE: Can use 'as you know' as a discourse marker of shared knowledge.
    e.g. I can't come to your wedding because, as you know, I live in England and on Thursday 18th July I'm going to take the FCE exam.
[DISCOURSE MARKERS/discourse markers in writing] FORM/USE: 'SO', SUMMARISING, INFORMAL: Can use 'so' as a discourse marker to summarise, usually in informal contexts.
    e.g. So, what do you think?
[REPORTED SPEECH/reported speech] FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'SAY', PRONOUN SHIFT: Can report statements using a reporting clause with 'say' + 'that-'clause, with a pronoun shift where relevant.
    e.g. My mother said that she can take us there.
[REPORTED SPEECH/reported speech] FORM: REPORTED STATEMENTS WITH 'TELL', PRONOUN SHIFT: Can report statements using a reporting clause with 'tell' + direct object + 'that-'clause, with a pronoun shift where relevant.
    e.g. [about a mobile phone] It's very important to me to have it with me because my brother is in the hospital and my father told me that he will call.
[FOCUS/focus] FORM/USE: PREPOSITIONAL PHRASES, FRONTING: Can use prepositional phrases in the front position for focus.
    e.g. In the morning, I go to the beach.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: AUXILIARY VERBS 'BE', 'HAVE', PRESENT: Can form negative statements of main verbs in the present continuous and present perfect with 'be' and 'have' + 'not/n't'.  ► present continuous ► present perfect
    e.g. The gas is not working.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: AUXILIARY VERB 'DO', PAST: Can form negative statements of main verbs in the past simple with 'didn't' + main verb. ► past simple
    e.g. It was very sad that you didn't come to my birthday party but it's ok.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: 'DO', IMPERATIVES: Can form negative imperatives of main verbs with 'don't' + main verb. ► Clauses: imperatives
    e.g. Don't forget your electric guitar.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: MODAL VERBS: Can use negative forms of modal verbs. ► modal verbs
    e.g. I'm sorry I can't come to your wedding because I am very sick.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: PRONOUNS: Can use a limited range of determiners as pronouns and indefinite pronouns in negative contexts.  ► Pronouns: quantity
    e.g. I bought a shirt, because I haven't got any.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: DETERMINERS 'NO': Can use determiner 'no' with nouns.  ► Determiners: quantity
    e.g. I bought these clothes because I need them for my new job, but now I have no money because they cost £250.
[NEGATION/negation] FORM: DETERMINERS 'ANY', 'MUCH', 'MANY': Can use determiners 'any', 'much' and 'many' with nouns in negative contexts. ► Determiners: quantity
    e.g. I bought them because I didn't have any shirts or hats.
[MODALITY/could] FORM: AFFIRMATIVE: Can use the affirmative form.
    e.g. You could come by bus, and we could meet at the bus station.
[MODALITY/could] FORM: NEGATIVE: Can use the negative form.
    e.g. [talking about a party] Too bad you couldn't come.
[MODALITY/could] FORM: QUESTIONS: Can use the question form.
    e.g. Could you help me?
[MODALITY/could] USE: ABILITY: Can use the negative form of 'could' to talk about inability.
    e.g. [talking about the key to an office] I couldn't work without it.
[MODALITY/could] USE: REQUESTS: Can use 'could' to make requests.
    e.g. Could you help me to paint my bedroom?
[MODALITY/could] USE: SUGGESTIONS: Can use 'could' with a limited range of verbs to make suggestions.
    e.g. We could have dinner after that, if you want.
B1:
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: BEFORE THE NOUN: Can use a comma to combine two adjectives used before the noun, following the usual order of adjective types.
    e.g. It was the biggest TV in the bazaar, with its huge, black screen.
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMBINING COMPARATIVE ADJECTIVES WITH 'AND': Can use 'and' to join a limited range of comparative adjectives.► adjectives: comparatives
    e.g. In the winter you can go to the fitness centre, there you can get stronger and fitter.
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMBINING MORE THAN TWO ADJECTIVES: Can use commas and 'and' to join more than two adjectives, after 'be'.
    e.g. Her name is Cassia, she is beautiful, intelligent, funny and very reliable.
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMBINING THE SAME COMPARATIVE ADJECTIVE WITH 'AND': Can use 'and' to repeat a comparative adjective to indicate change over time, usually after 'become' or get. ► adjectives: comparatives
    e.g. I could see how the fire was burning the door, and the room became hotter and hotter.
[ADJECTIVES/combining] FORM: COMPOUND ADJECTIVES: Can use a limited range of compound adjectives ('good-looking', 'well-known')
    e.g. He was tall and good-looking.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM/USE: WITH '(SO) MUCH': Can use '(so) much' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be'.
    e.g. I think the teacher in the new class is much better than the last one.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM/USE: WITH 'A (LITTLE) BIT': Can use 'a (little) bit' to modify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be'.
    e.g. He's 15, a bit smaller than me, dark hair, brown eyes.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM/USE: WITH 'AND': Can use 'and' to repeat a comparative adjective to indicate change over time, usually after 'become' or 'get'.  ► adjectives: comparatives
    e.g. I saw everything become smaller and smaller.
[ADJECTIVES/comparatives] FORM/USE: WITH 'EVEN': Can use 'even' to modify and intensify comparative adjectives used predicatively after a verb, usually 'be' and 'get'.
    e.g. [talking about a series of films] I'm sure you've seen part one already, but part two was even better!
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: ADJECTIVE PHRASE + NOUN: Can use adjective phrases attributively, before a noun.
    e.g. … my boss told me that at 10.15 am I have a very important meeting.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'ENOUGH': Can use 'enough' to modify adjectives.
    e.g. My room is big enough, there is a lot of space, and for this reason I will buy a stereo.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'QUITE A': Can form adjective phrases with 'quite a' + adjective.
    e.g. I been thinking about your last letter for quite a long time and I came to the following conclusion.
[ADJECTIVES/modifying] FORM: WITH 'TOO' + 'TO'-INFINITIVE: Can use 'too' before adjectives followed by 'to'-infinitive.
    e.g. He always told me that he was too busy to write a letter to me, his wife.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: ADJECTIVES WITH PREFIX 'A-': Can use adjectives with the prefix a- ('asleep', 'awake', 'alive', 'alone') predicatively only, after linking verbs.
    e.g. I was very nervious because I was alone at the bus station …
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: ATTRIBUTIVE (WITH NOUNS): Can use an increasing range of adjectives attributively, before a noun.
► noun phrases
    e.g. The main character in the programme is a journalist who is visiting the town.
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: WITH 'MAKE' AS OBJECT COMPLEMENT: Can use adjectives as object complement after 'make'.
    e.g. That's something that makes me angry!
[ADJECTIVES/position] FORM: WITH PRONOUN: Can use an adjective or adjective phrase after pronouns like 'something', 'nothing', 'somewhere', 'nowhere'.
    e.g. I like cooking and I want to cook something special.
[ADJECTIVES/superlatives] FORM/USE: COMPLEX NOUN PHRASES: Can form an increasing range of complex noun phrases with a superlative adjective + prepositional phrase, to talk about something unique. ► noun phrases
    e.g. Yesterday was the most exciting day of my life!
